text: 4 Descartes’ thoughts on the soul must again be understood against the background of the seventeenth century debates about ‘passions’ and how to account for them. In The Passions of the Soul, Descartes distinguishes in articles 24 and 25 between passions which relate to the body (like heat and cold) and passions which relate to the soul, i.e. phenomena like perceptions and emotions which are ‘moved by spirits’ (article 26). But in order to remove any hint of extra-physiological reasoning about feelings and spirits, in article 31 Descartes states that the soul is situated in the pineal gland. Liminality and the Modern 124 and desire. Aristotle equally argued for a composite nature of the human soul encompassing rational and emotive dimensions. But this composite and multi- layered anima was exactly what Descartes wanted to get rid of. 5 Descartes’ real break with Aristotle was therefore not just the placing of the self outside body, but also the turning of the self into mind. The self-reform that Descartes brought about was directed exclusively towards logos: his finding of truth was a matter of knowledge as it can be established on the basis of the individual rational mind, and on nothing outside of that. 6 Therefore, the ‘inside’ that Descartes explored and then publicly declared was of a very special quality. His philosophy was a tearing apart of components, an outstanding example of maximum orderliness and analytic capacity to strip matters down to their smallest components: an exercise in anatomy. It is reason as dry and soulless as can be, as should be. The order that Descartes found was an order of the mind; the truth he found was a truth of the thinking self. Descartes’ Prophecy This is a first radical difference from religious prophecy, as discussed by Weber (1978: 439–67). The self to Descartes has nothing to do with soul in either the ancient Greek or Christian traditions. In the latter, the notion of the soul, in spite of historical developments of the concept, has always been connected to transcendental experiences, without which we do not become really human. Whenever Descartes discusses the word ‘soul’ he uses it as a synonym for ‘mind’ or ‘thought’, that which goes on in the brain. Before Descartes it was commonplace to regard the soul the animating principle of the human person, in contrast to the body understood as a biological mechanism. In The Passions of the Soul, Descartes states with medical certainty 7 that the soul operates from a ‘certain very small gland’ situated in the middle of the brain (articles 31 and 32; in Descartes 1997: 372). The mind may be separate from the body, but its functioning can be approached with a mechanistic, naturalistic-deductive model. 5 Although in Part II of The Passions of the Soul Descartes lists six primitive passions (wonder, love, hate, desire, joy and sadness), he is very consciously rejecting any real distinction between parts of the soul, such as the Platonic distinction between the rational, concupiscible and irascible appetities. For a fuller discussion, see Gaukroger (1995: 384–417). 6 Descartes represents, also in this important sense, a rupture with the use of meditative practices as developed in the philosophical traditions of late antiquity and early Christianity (as in Augustine). Descartes’ meditations are exactly not spiritual (following the work by Hadot 1995), as they confine the scope of human searching to cognitive sensing and logical operations upon primary concepts and ideas. 7 Descartes’ interest in anatomy and physiology goes back to the late 1620s. He studied dissected animal bodies, and in a letter to Mersenne from 1632 he tells that having investigated the body’s life-functions, he is now successfully dissecting animal heads to explain mental phenomena like imagination and memory (Gaukroger 1995: 227–8). Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 125 Descartes’ system therefore produces a crucial narrowing of the problem of being, rather than – as prophecy normally provokes – a differentiation of soul via a differentiation of consciousness and human experiences. 8 Mind may provide the basis of an ordered universe, but human experiences outside the mind remain undifferentiated and unordered. From Descartes onwards the self becomes mind. The mind rejects the environment and finds its path forwards through thoughtful reflection upon itself. Descartes claimed to have separated the evils of his day, which were the unreliable nature of bodily sensation, of mental habit and tradition. The Cartesian cogito resulted in a belief in, and practice of, self as the origin of knowledge and truth that came to dominate Western philosophy. It is of course true that Kant and Hume both claimed to have refuted Descartes, but to an important extent they carried on the premises of the problem, namely that the sources of truth and untruth were somehow located in the self. Here lies a second difference from traditional prophecy: Descartes’ truth- revelation did not start with a transcendental vision. It is from the existence of the thinking self, the cogito, that Descartes proved the existence of God. The structure of revelation goes from the solitary self as the basis of thinking to the existence of God. Descartes argued deductively that since he as a human being with a body was not perfect but still had the idea of perfection, that idea must exist somewhere. That somewhere, he claimed in Discourse on Method, was God (Descartes 1999: 26). The grace he found, even as to the existence of God, was purely cognitive. Descartes’ vision is not a vision of divinity from whom human thinking and reason finds its place. It goes the other way round: human thinking is the vision into which divinity must enter, and it enters exactly insofar as it fulfils the conditions of logical reason. If Descartes is ‘the philosopher of modernity’ it cannot be because he clears new ground for safe knowledge (even when taken on its own premises his theory was definitively refuted by Kant). Descartes should rather be considered the founder of modern philosophy in the sense that he is the
